title: My own claim or representative
h1: Claiming on your own or on behalf of someone else
intro: You can claim for yourself or on behalf of someone, or you can claim for a group of people.

claimingSection.heading: Claiming on your own or with others
claimingSection.p1: You can make a claim to an employment tribunal on your own, where you are the only claimant. You can also make a claim with another person, or a group of people against the same employer or organisation if you share similar issues in fact and law.
claimingSection.p2: If you’re claiming with other people, we only need their basic personal details. We do not need details about their claim.

representativeSection.heading: Representatives
representativeSection.p1: A representative is someone who can act for you to complete the claim, write to the employment tribunal or present your case at a hearing.
representativeSection.p2: You do not need a representative to make a claim. You can add a representative at any point if you want to.

detailsSummary1: Who can act as a representative

freeList: trade unions (if one is available to you)
advisors from law clinics or law centres
detailsText1.p2: Paid representation can be with legal professionals, including:
paidList: claims management companies (regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA))

detailsSummary2: How to get a legal representative

detailsText2.p1: The following links open in a new tab

englandWales.linkText: Find a solicitor in England and Wales
englandWales.description: list of solicitors run by The Law Society

scotland.description: database of solicitors run by The Law Society of Scotland

fru.description: charity providing legal advice, case preparation and advocacy for people not eligible for legal aid or who cannot afford lawyers (this service is not available in Scotland)

sulcn.p1: In Scotland, some university law clinics can provide advice and representation. You can find a list at
sulcn.linkText: The Scottish University Law Clinic Network (SULCN)

links.lawCentres: local ‘law centres’ - not-for-profit legal practices providing legal aid for people who cannot afford lawyers

p2.t1: A trade union may be able to pay for a solicitor and free legal advice is available from

detailsText2.p4: Some people may also be able to get help to pay for legal advice through legal aid.

question.legend: Who are you making a claim for?
question.hint: If you do not know, select ‘I'm claiming for myself’
question.radio1Hint: including making a claim alone or with others
question.radio2: I'm claiming on behalf of someone else
question.radio3: I'm a legal representative representing a claimant

claimantRepresentedQuestion.required: Tell us if you are representing yourself or acting as a representative
